Critics such as [Noam Chomsky](/wiki/Noam_Chomsky "Noam Chomsky") argue continuing research into symbolic AI will still be necessary to attain general intelligence,[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangley2011-446)[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKatz2012-447) in part because sub-symbolic AI is a move away from [explainable AI](/wiki/Explainable_AI "Explainable AI"): it can be difficult or impossible to understand why a modern statistical AI program made a particular decision. The emerging field of [neuro-symbolic artificial intelligence](/wiki/Neuro-symbolic_AI "Neuro-symbolic AI") attempts to bridge the two approaches. #### Neat vs. scruffy Main article: [Neats and scruffies](/wiki/Neats_and_scruffies "Neats and scruffies") "Neats" hope that intelligent behavior is described using simple, elegant principles (such as [logic](/wiki/Logic "Logic"), [optimization](/wiki/Optimization "Optimization"), or [neural networks](/wiki/Artificial_neural_network "Artificial neural network")). "Scruffies" expect that it necessarily requires solving a large number of unrelated problems.

Neats defend their programs with theoretical rigor, scruffies rely mainly on incremental testing to see if they work. This issue was actively discussed in the 1970s and 1980s,[[420]](#cite_note-448) but eventually was seen as irrelevant. Modern AI has elements of both. #### Soft vs. hard computing Main article: [Soft computing](/wiki/Soft_computing "Soft computing") Finding a provably correct or optimal solution is [intractable](/wiki/Intractability_(complexity) "Intractability (complexity)") for many important problems.[[15]](#cite_note-Intractability_and_efficiency_and_the_combinatorial_explosion-17) Soft computing is a set of techniques, including [genetic algorithms](/wiki/Genetic_algorithms "Genetic algorithms"), [fuzzy logic](/wiki/Fuzzy_logic "Fuzzy logic") and neural networks, that are tolerant of imprecision, uncertainty, partial truth and approximation.

Soft computing was introduced in the late 1980s and most successful AI programs in the 21st century are examples of soft computing with neural networks. #### Narrow vs. general AI Main articles: [Weak artificial intelligence](/wiki/Weak_artificial_intelligence "Weak artificial intelligence") and [Artificial general intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence "Artificial general intelligence") AI researchers are divided as to whether to pursue the goals of artificial general intelligence and [superintelligence](/wiki/Superintelligence "Superintelligence") directly or to solve as many specific problems as possible (narrow AI) in hopes these solutions will lead indirectly to the field's long-term goals.[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPennachinGoertzel2007-449)[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2016-450) General intelligence is difficult to define and difficult to measure, and modern AI has had more verifiable successes by focusing on specific problems with specific solutions. The sub-field of artificial general intelligence studies this area exclusively. ### Machine consciousness, sentience, and mind Main articles: [Philosophy of artificial intelligence](/wiki/Philosophy_of_artificial_intelligence "Philosophy of artificial intelligence") and [Artificial consciousness](/wiki/Artificial_consciousness "Artificial consciousness") There is no settled consensus in [philosophy of mind](/wiki/Philosophy_of_mind "Philosophy of mind") on whether a machine can have a [mind](/wiki/Mind "Mind"), [consciousness](/wiki/Consciousness "Consciousness") and [mental states](/wiki/Philosophy_of_mind "Philosophy of mind") in the same sense that human beings do. This issue considers the internal experiences of the machine, rather than its external behavior.

Mainstream AI research considers this issue irrelevant because it does not affect the goals of the field: to build machines that can solve problems using intelligence. [Russell](/wiki/Stuart_J._Russell "Stuart J. Russell") and [Norvig](/wiki/Norvig "Norvig") add that "[t]he additional project of making a machine conscious in exactly the way humans are is not one that we are equipped to take on."[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussellNorvig2021986-451) However, the question has become central to the philosophy of mind. It is also typically the central question at issue in [artificial intelligence in fiction](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_in_fiction "Artificial intelligence in fiction"). #### Consciousness Main articles: [Hard problem of consciousness](/wiki/Hard_problem_of_consciousness "Hard problem of consciousness") and [Theory of mind](/wiki/Theory_of_mind "Theory of mind") [David Chalmers](/wiki/David_Chalmers "David Chalmers") identified two problems in understanding the mind, which he named the "hard" and "easy" problems of consciousness.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChalmers1995-452) The easy problem is understanding how the brain processes signals, makes plans and controls behavior.

The hard problem is explaining how this *feels* or why it should feel like anything at all, assuming we are right in thinking that it truly does feel like something (Dennett's consciousness illusionism says this is an illusion). While human [information processing](/wiki/Information_processing_(psychology) "Information processing (psychology)") is easy to explain, human [subjective experience](/wiki/Subjective_experience "Subjective experience") is difficult to explain. For example, it is easy to imagine a color-blind person who has learned to identify which objects in their field of view are red, but it is not clear what would be required for the person to *know what red looks like*.[[425]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennett1991-453) #### Computationalism and functionalism Main articles: [Computational theory of mind](/wiki/Computational_theory_of_mind "Computational theory of mind") and [Functionalism (philosophy of mind)](/wiki/Functionalism_(philosophy_of_mind) "Functionalism (philosophy of mind)") Computationalism is the position in the [philosophy of mind](/wiki/Philosophy_of_mind "Philosophy of mind") that the human mind is an information processing system and that thinking is a form of computing.

Computationalism argues that the relationship between mind and body is similar or identical to the relationship between software and hardware and thus may be a solution to the [mind–body problem](/wiki/Mind%E2%80%93body_problem "Mind–body problem"). This philosophical position was inspired by the work of AI researchers and cognitive scientists in the 1960s and was originally proposed by philosophers [Jerry Fodor](/wiki/Jerry_Fodor "Jerry Fodor") and [Hilary Putnam](/wiki/Hilary_Putnam "Hilary Putnam").[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorst2005-454) Philosopher [John Searle](/wiki/John_Searle "John Searle") characterized this position as "[strong AI](/wiki/Strong_AI_hypothesis "Strong AI hypothesis")": "The appropriately programmed computer with the right inputs and outputs would thereby have a mind in exactly the same sense human beings have minds."[[ac]](#cite_note-Searle's_strong_AI-458) Searle challenges this claim with his [Chinese room](/wiki/Chinese_room "Chinese room") argument, which attempts to show that even a computer capable of perfectly simulating human behavior would not have a mind.[[430]](#cite_note-459) #### AI welfare and rights It is difficult or impossible to reliably evaluate whether an advanced [AI is sentient](/wiki/Sentient_AI "Sentient AI") (has the ability to feel), and if so, to what degree.[[431]](#cite_note-460) But if there is a significant chance that a given machine can feel and suffer, then it may be entitled to certain rights or welfare protection measures, similarly to animals.[[432]](#cite_note-Thomson-2022-461)[[433]](#cite_note-Kateman-2023-462) [Sapience](/wiki/Sapience "Sapience") (a set of capacities related to high intelligence, such as discernment or [self-awareness](/wiki/Self-awareness "Self-awareness")) may provide another moral basis for AI rights.[[432]](#cite_note-Thomson-2022-461) [Robot rights](/wiki/Robot_rights "Robot rights") are also sometimes proposed as a practical way to integrate autonomous agents into society.[[434]](#cite_note-463) In 2017, the European Union considered granting "electronic personhood" to some of the most capable AI systems. Similarly to the legal status of companies, it would have conferred rights but also responsibilities.[[435]](#cite_note-464) Critics argued in 2018 that granting rights to AI systems would downplay the importance of [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"), and that legislation should focus on user needs rather than speculative futuristic scenarios.

They also noted that robots lacked the autonomy to take part in society on their own.[[436]](#cite_note-465)[[437]](#cite_note-466) Progress in AI increased interest in the topic. Proponents of AI welfare and rights often argue that AI sentience, if it emerges, would be particularly easy to deny. They warn that this may be a [moral blind spot](/wiki/Moral_blindness "Moral blindness") analogous to [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") or [factory farming](/wiki/Factory_farming "Factory farming"), which could lead to [large-scale suffering](/wiki/Suffering_risks "Suffering risks") if sentient AI is created and carelessly exploited.[[433]](#cite_note-Kateman-2023-462)[[432]](#cite_note-Thomson-2022-461) ## Future ### Superintelligence and the singularity A [superintelligence](/wiki/Superintelligence "Superintelligence") is a hypothetical agent that would possess intelligence far surpassing that of the brightest and most gifted human mind.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2016-450) If research into [artificial general intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence "Artificial general intelligence") produced sufficiently intelligent software, it might be able to [reprogram and improve itself](/wiki/Recursive_self-improvement "Recursive self-improvement").
The improved software would be even better at improving itself, leading to what [I. J. Good](/wiki/I._J._Good "I.

J. Good") called an "[intelligence explosion](/wiki/Intelligence_explosion "Intelligence explosion")" and [Vernor Vinge](/wiki/Vernor_Vinge "Vernor Vinge") called a "[singularity](/wiki/Technological_singularity "Technological singularity")".[[438]](#cite_note-467) However, technologies cannot improve exponentially indefinitely, and typically follow an [S-shaped curve](/wiki/S-shaped_curve "S-shaped curve"), slowing when they reach the physical limits of what the technology can do.[[439]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussellNorvig20211005-468) ### Transhumanism Main article: [Transhumanism](/wiki/Transhumanism "Transhumanism") Robot designer [Hans Moravec](/wiki/Hans_Moravec "Hans Moravec"), cyberneticist [Kevin Warwick](/wiki/Kevin_Warwick "Kevin Warwick") and inventor [Ray Kurzweil](/wiki/Ray_Kurzweil "Ray Kurzweil") have predicted that humans and machines may merge in the future into [cyborgs](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg") that are more capable and powerful than either. This idea, called transhumanism, has roots in the writings of [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley") and [Robert Ettinger](/wiki/Robert_Ettinger "Robert Ettinger").[[440]](#cite_note-469) [Edward Fredkin](/wiki/Edward_Fredkin "Edward Fredkin") argues that "artificial intelligence is the next step in evolution", an idea first proposed by [Samuel Butler](/wiki/Samuel_Butler_(novelist) "Samuel Butler (novelist)")'s "[Darwin among the Machines](/wiki/Darwin_among_the_Machines "Darwin among the Machines")" as far back as 1863, and expanded upon by [George Dyson](/wiki/George_Dyson_(science_historian) "George Dyson (science historian)") in his 1998 book *[Darwin Among the Machines: The Evolution of Global Intelligence](/wiki/Darwin_Among_the_Machines#Evolution_of_Global_Intelligence "Darwin Among the Machines")*.[[441]](#cite_note-470) ## In fiction Main article: [Artificial intelligence in fiction](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_in_fiction "Artificial intelligence in fiction") [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Capek_play.jpg/330px-Capek_play.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capek_play.jpg) The word "robot" itself was coined by [Karel Čapek](/wiki/Karel_%C4%8Capek "Karel Čapek") in his 1921 play *[R.U.R.](/wiki/R.U.R. "R.U.R.")*, the title standing for "Rossum's Universal Robots".

Thought-capable artificial beings have appeared as storytelling devices since antiquity,[[442]](#cite_note-AI_in_myth-471) and have been a persistent theme in [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction").[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCorduck2004340–400-472) A common [trope](/wiki/Trope_(literature) "Trope (literature)") in these works began with [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley")'s *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")*, where a human creation becomes a threat to its masters. This includes such works as [Arthur C. Clarke's](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(novel) "2001: A Space Odyssey (novel)") and [Stanley Kubrick's](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey "2001: A Space Odyssey") *2001: A Space Odyssey* (both 1968), with [HAL 9000](/wiki/HAL_9000 "HAL 9000"), the murderous computer in charge of the *[Discovery One](/wiki/Discovery_One "Discovery One")* spaceship, as well as *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")* (1882), *[The Terminator](/wiki/The_Terminator "The Terminator")* (1984) and *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix "The Matrix")* (1999).

In contrast, the rare loyal robots such as Gort from *[The Day the Earth Stood Still](/wiki/The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still "The Day the Earth Stood Still")* (1951) and Bishop from *[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(film) "Aliens (film)")* (1986) are less prominent in popular culture.[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButtazzo2001-473) [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") introduced the [Three Laws of Robotics](/wiki/Three_Laws_of_Robotics "Three Laws of Robotics") in many stories, most notably with the "[Multivac](/wiki/Multivac "Multivac")" super-intelligent computer. Asimov's laws are often brought up during lay discussions of machine ethics;[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson2008-474) while almost all artificial intelligence researchers are familiar with Asimov's laws through popular culture, they generally consider the laws useless for many reasons, one of which is their ambiguity.[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCauley2007-475) Several works use AI to force us to confront the fundamental question of what makes us human, showing us artificial beings that have [the ability to feel](/wiki/Sentience "Sentience"), and thus to suffer. This appears in [Karel Čapek](/wiki/Karel_%C4%8Capek "Karel Čapek")'s *[R.U.R.](/wiki/R.U.R. "R.U.R.")*, the films *[A.I.

Artificial Intelligence](/wiki/A.I._Artificial_Intelligence "A.I. Artificial Intelligence")* and *[Ex Machina](/wiki/Ex_Machina_(film) "Ex Machina (film)")*, as well as the novel *[Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?](/wiki/Do_Androids_Dream_of_Electric_Sheep%3F "Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?")*, by [Philip K. Dick](/wiki/Philip_K._Dick "Philip K.

Dick"). Dick considers the idea that our understanding of human subjectivity is altered by technology created with artificial intelligence.[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalvan1997-476) ## See also * [Artificial
